title: Hilbert's 10th Problem Over Rings of Integers
speaker: Ari Shnidman
abstract: We show that for every quadratic extension of number fields K/F, there exists an abelian variety A/F of positive rank whose rank does not grow upon base change to K. By work of Shlapentokh, this implies that Hilbert's tenth problem over the ring of integers R of any number field has a negative solution. That is, there does not exist an algorithm to determine whether a polynomial equation in several variables over R has solutions in R. This is joint work with Levent Alpöge, Manjul Bhargava, and Wei Ho.

proof_stages.label: Diophantine-definability reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls that undecidability of Hilbert's tenth problem over a ring of integers follows from making the ordinary integers Diophantine there, and then invokes Shlapentokh's abelian-variety criterion for producing such Diophantine definitions.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic-extension target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Shlapentokh's reduction shows that it is enough to handle quadratic extensions, so the main theorem is stated: for every quadratic K/F there is an abelian variety over F of positive rank whose rank does not increase over K.

proof_stages.label: Cyclotomic reduction and chosen Jacobians
proof_stages.summary: After adjoining an l-th root of unity and descending by Weil restriction, the proof works with the Jacobians J_n of the curves y^2=x^l+n and records their quadratic and μ_l-twists together with the rank formula for quadratic base change.

proof_stages.label: Phi-Selmer descent and silent primes
proof_stages.summary: The isogeny phi=1-zeta is used to define Selmer groups that bound Mordell-Weil rank, and inert or ramified primes in K/F are shown to be silent because the relevant local cohomology vanishes.

proof_stages.label: Flexible zero-Selmer twists
proof_stages.summary: Using Yu's refinement of Mazur-Rubin, an initial μ_l-twist with trivial phi-Selmer group is found, and a local-condition comparison shows that many further σ-unit twists still have trivial Selmer group.

proof_stages.label: Infinite unit-equation parameter set
proof_stages.summary: A set X of twist parameters t_{a,b} is defined by a σ-unit equation 2a+2rb=1 together with congruence conditions, and a number-field generalization of Vander Corput's prime-equation theorem is used to prove that X is infinite.

proof_stages.label: Non-torsion points on the quadratic twists
proof_stages.summary: A torsion-avoidance lemma and the unit-equation relation are combined to write explicit points on the quadratic twists J_{r^2t^2}, and all but finitely many of these points have infinite order.

proof_stages.label: Rank comparison and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: Choosing A=J_{r^2t^2}, the proof combines the positive rank just produced with the zero rank of its quadratic twist to get rank A(K)=rank A(F)>0, completing the theorem and hence the Hilbert-tenth corollary.

examples.name: Pell equation as the prototype
examples.note: The Pell equation is recalled as the prototype in which an infinite rank-one group of solutions makes integers Diophantine, motivating the later replacement by positive-rank abelian varieties.
examples.engagement_reason: The Pell equation is invoked only as the prototype/analogy for using an infinite rank-one group in a Diophantine definition, with no calculation or construction carried out on it.

passes.reason: Pell equations form a standard infinite family used here as the prototype for producing Diophantine definitions via infinitely many points.
Pell’s equation is a genus-zero norm equation serving as a minimal prototype, not a refutation.
A Pell equation is used as a standard infinite-family prototype for Diophantine definability, not as a failure case.
The Pell equation is used as the standard positive-rank prototype for Diophantine definability, not as a sporadic, degenerate, or refuting example.
the Pell equation is used as the rank-one positive-rank prototype, with its infinite cyclic solution group being minimal positive rank rather than a refutation.
Pell equations are used as a standard prototype for Diophantine definability via an infinite cyclic solution group, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Forcing points on a hyperelliptic curve by twisting
examples.note: The generic example y^2=f(x), where one chooses x and then quadratically twists to get a rational point, illustrates why producing points alone is much easier than simultaneously controlling rank.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly runs the twisting construction on the hyperelliptic curves, defines the twists, solves a unit equation, writes down a resulting point, and checks it gives positive rank.

passes.reason: The hyperelliptic curves and their twists are standard members of an infinite family, with twisting used to manufacture rational points.

examples.name: Vander Corput three-term progressions in primes
examples.note: The classical theorem on three-term arithmetic progressions in primes is cited as the over-Z model for the generalized prime-solution input that yields many unit-equation solutions.
examples.engagement_reason: Vander Corput’s three-term progressions are cited as an input/generalization for producing unit-equation solutions, but no progression is constructed or proved.
